summary: Gruber dismantles Eric Jackson's Forbes article urging Apple to aggressively acquire companies in the wake of Facebook's $16B WhatsApp purchase. He argues this advice reflects complete ignorance of Apple's DNA, which is built on focus and saying no rather than conglomeration. Gruber concedes that Tesla is the one acquisition suggestion that makes sense, since it could integrate into Apple's core business and shares the battery engineering challenge. He contrasts the conglomerate model that might work for Facebook or GE with Apple's integration-focused acquisition strategy, citing PA Semi as the exemplar.
key_points: Jackson's advice to 'play offense' through aggressive M&A fundamentally misunderstands Apple's focus-driven business model
Tesla is the only suggested acquisition that makes strategic sense, due to product integration potential and shared battery engineering challenges
Apple's acquisition philosophy is integration, not conglomeration — PA Semi's $278M purchase exemplifies this by enabling Apple's mobile chip dominance
Conglomeration may work for Facebook or GE but would be a disaster for Apple's focused product strategy
Apple already has iMessage as its messaging platform, making a WhatsApp-style acquisition pointless
money_quotes: This is the worst sort of advice, suggesting a complete ignorance of everything Apple stands for.
Just buy something. Spend, spend, spend. Acquire. Buy all the spaghetti, throw it against the wall, see what sticks.
Apple wasn't joking about 'a thousand no's for every yes' — that's really how they think, what they believe. That's the DNA.
Conglomeration may well work out well for Facebook. General Electric has done well with that model for over 100 years. But it would be a disaster for Apple.
PA Semi — a chump change $278 million acquisition that laid the groundwork for Apple to become the leading mobile semiconductor company in the world.
key_insight: Apple's strength lies in disciplined focus and acquisitions for integration, not the scattershot conglomeration that pundits perpetually urge upon them.
